source.ru: Древнеисландский текст, ред. Haraldur Bernharðsson. Текст на русском языке в переводе А. И. Корсуна, СЭ–1963, с. 104–108.
source.en: An ancient Icelandic text, edited by Haraldur Bernharðsson. Translation by Henry Adams Bellows.

lines: I Sigurðr ok Reginn fóru upp á Gnitaheiði ok hittu þar slóð Fáfnis þá er hann skreið til vatns. Þar gjörði Sigurðr gröf mikla á veginum ok gekk Sigurðr þar í. En er Fáfnir skreið af gullinu blés hann eitri ok hraut það fyr ofan höfuð Sigurði. En er Fáfnir skreið yfir gröfina þá lagði Sigurðr hann með sverði til hjarta. Fáfnir hristi sik ok barði höfði ok sporði. Sigurðr hljóp ór gröfinni ok sá þá hvárr annan. Fáfnir kvað:
II Sigurðr dulði nafns síns fyr því að það var trúa þeira í forneskju að orð feigs mannz mætti mikið ef hann bölvaði óvin sínum með nafni. Hann kvað:
«Veizt u ef föður né átt at
af hverju vart u undri alinn?»
er hef k þik vápnum vegið.»
þú fannt að ek lauss lifi!»
en ek þér satt eitt segi-k:
þér verða þeir baugar að bana!»
fann k a ek marga mögu.»
þér verða þeir baugar að bana!»
til þess gullz er í lyngvi liggr,
III Reginn var á brott horfinn meðan Sigurðr vá Fáfni ok kom þá aftr er Sigurðr strauk ‖ blóð af sverðinu. Reginn kvað:
er þú þerrir Gram á grasi;
ok veld ek þó sjálfr sumu.»
IV Þá gekk Reginn að Fáfni ok skar hjarta ór hánum með sverði er Rið­ill heitir ok þá drakk hann blóð ór undinni eftir.
ok halt Fáfnis hjarta við funa!
meðan ek á Fáfni rauð k

V Sigurðr tók Fáfnis hjarta ok stei­kði á teini. Er hann hugði að full­ steikt væri ok freyddi sveitinn ór hjartanu þá tók hann á fin­gri sínum ok skynjaði hvárt full­steikt væri. Hann brann ok brá fingrinum í munn sér. En er hjart­blóð Fáfnis kom á tungu há­num ok skildi hann fuglsrödd. Hann heyrði að igður klökuðu á hrí­si­num. Igðan kvað:
fjölð, því er und Fáfni lá.»
VI Sigurðr hjó höfuð af Regin ok þá át hann Fáfnis hjarta ok drakk blóð þeira beggja, Regins ok Fáfnis. Þá heyrði Sigurðr hvað igður mæltu:

VII Sigurðr reið eftir slóð Fáfnis til bælis hans ok fann það opið ok hurðir af járni ok gætti; af járni vóru ok allir timbrstokkar í húsinu en grafið í jörð niðr. Þar fann Sigurðr stórmikið gull ok fylldi þar tvær kistur. Þar tók hann ægis hjálm ok gullbrynju ok sverðið Hrotta ok marga dýrgripi ok klyfjaði þar með Grana. En hestrinn vildi eigi fram ganga fyrr en Sigurðr steig á bak hánum.

I Сигурд и Регин отправились на Гнитахейд и нашли там след Фавнира, который он оставил, когда полз к водопою. Сигурд вырыл большую яму возле следа и засел в ней. И когда Фавнир пополз от сокровища, он изрыгал яд, и яд падал на голову Сигурда. И когда Фавнир проползал над ямой, Сигурд вонзил ему в сердце меч. Фавнир затрясся и стал бить головой и хвостом. Сигурд выскочил из ямы, и они увидели друг друга. Фавнир сказал:
II Сигурд скрыл свое имя, потому что в древние времена верили, что слова умирающего могущественны, если он проклинает своего недруга, называя его по имени. Он сказал:
III Регина не было, когда Сигурд убивал Фавнира. Он вернулся, когда Сигурд вытирал кровь с меча. Регин сказал:
IV Тогда Регин подошел к Фавниру и вырезал у него сердце ме­чом, который называется Ридиль. Затем он стал пить кровь из раны.

V Сигурд взял сердце Фавнира и стал поджаривать его на палочке. Когда он решил, что оно изжарилось, и кровь из сердца запенилась, он дотронулся до него пальцем, чтобы узнать, готово ли оно. Он обжегся и поднес палец ко рту. Но когда кровь из сердца Фавнира попала ему на язык, он стал понимать птичью речь. Он услышал, как щебечут синицы в кустах. Синица сказала:
VI Сигурд отрубил голову Реги­ну. Затем он отведал сердца Фавнира и отпил крови обоих — Регина и Фавнира. Тогда Сигурд услышал, как синицы говорили:

VII Сигурд поехал по следу Фавнира в его логово и нашел его открытым, и двери были железными, и дверная рама тоже. Железными бы­ли также все балки в доме, и дом был закопан в землю. Там Сигурд нашел очень много золота и наполнил им два сундука. Там он взял шлем-страшило, золотую кольчугу, меч Хротти и много сокровищ и нагрузил всем этим Грани. Но конь не хотел идти, пока Сигурд не сел на него.

Sigurth and Regin went up to the Gnitaheith, and found there the track that Fafnir made when he crawled to water. Then Sigurth made a great trench across the path, and took his place therein. When Fafnir crawled from his gold, he blew out venom, and it ran down from above on Sigurth's head. But when Fafnir crawled over the trench, then Sigurth thrust his sword into his body to the heart. Fafnir writhed and struck out with his head and tail. Sigurth leaped from the trench, and each looked at the other. Fafnir said:
of whom then, youth, art thou born?
And struck thy sword to my heart."
Sigurth concealed his name because it was believed in olden times that the word of a dying man might have great power if he cursed his foe by his name. He said:
By what wonder wast thou born?
Thou knowest now thou dost lie.)"
Who smote thee thus with the sword."
My life to make me lose?
For bold in boyhood thou art."
And my shining sword so sharp;
One might see thee fiercely fight;
And to fear are prisoners prone."
The wealth that my father's was;
Thou hast found that free I live."
Yet truth alone do I tell;
And the rings thy bane shall be."
Till the destined day shall come;
Thou findest, and doom of a fool;
All danger is near to death."
And the babe from the mother bring?"
Nor one in race they were;
Shall seek the play of swords;
And the steeds shall swim in the flood."
While guarding my gold I lay;
For a fiercer never I found."
When he faces a valiant foe;
That he is not the bravest of all.,"
By the hoard my father had;
And weapons nor wiles I feared.)"
And hard didst show thy heart;
For him who owns the helm."
And the rings thy bane shall be."
To the gold in the heather hidden;
Lying where Hel shall have thee."
Us both to death will he bring;
For the mightier man wast thou."
Regin had gone to a distance while Sigurth fought Fafnir, and came back while Sigurth was wiping the blood from his sword. Regin said:
And Fafnir in fight hast slain;
In the blood of a foeman's breast."
As Gram with grass thou cleansest;
With his blood my blade so keen;
With the might of the dragon
While thou in the heather didst hide."
The keen-edged blade thou didst bear."
For him who shall fiercely fight;
Though dull his blade may be."
When the clash of battle comes;
Shall face what before him lies."
If thou hadst not mocked at my might."
Then Regin went up to Fafnir and cut out his heart with his sword, that was named Rithil, and then he drank blood from the wounds. Regin said:
Hold Fafnir's heart to the fire;
Since deep of blood I have drunk."

Sigurth took Fafnir's heart and cooked it on a spit. When he thought that it was fully cooked, and the blood foamed out of the heart, then he tried it with his finger to see whether it was fully cooked. He burned his finger, and put it in his mouth. But when Fafnir's heart's-blood came on his tongue, he understood the speech of birds. He heard nut-hatches chattering in the thickets. A nut hatch said:
His foe, the bane of the folk,
And let him of rings be robbed;
As the tale of my death to tell;
And hence to hell shall go."
Sigurth hewed off Regin's head, and then he ate Fafnir's heart, and drank the blood of both Regin and Fafnir. Then Sigurth heard what the nut-hatch said:
rings together, Not kingly is it
aught to fear; I know a maid,
there is none so fair, Rich in gold,

Sigurth rode along Fafnir's trail to his lair, and found it open. The gate-posts were of iron, and the gates; of iron, too, were all the beams in the house, which was dug down into the earth. There Sigurth found a mighty store of gold, and he filled two chests full thereof; he took the fear-helm and a golden mail-coat and the sword Hrotti, and many other precious things, and loaded Grani with them, but the horse would not go forward until Sigurth mounted on his back.
